fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester 4.0 › enhanced XML support › emitting XML and resolving namespaces › a worked example

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The following code causes MathML to be emitted (in the correct namespace), combining and :

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester 4.0 › enhanced XML support › emitting XML and resolving namespaces

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Since version 3.0, Forester’s source language has supported a limited form of custom XML output by means of the \xml command. This support was, however, somewhat unwieldy: no built-in accounting for XML namespaces was included, so correct usage of \xml was prohibitively difficult because of the need for xmlns attributes on essentially every node.

Forester treats XML namespace prefixes in a way that respects \alpha-equivalence: in other words, any prefix mentioned must be bound, and it is possible for two different textually identical namespace prefixes to denote different namespaces depending on their scope. This makes it possible to compose code that emits XML without coordinating namespace prefixes, which I have found to be a major impediment to the correct use of XML for non-trivial work.
An XML namespace prefix is bound by a declaration like the following:
This declaration has the same scoping rules as \def.
The old \xml syntax has been replaced with a new convenient notation for XML literals that supports declared XML namespace prefixes. The grammar of XML literals is summarised below:
For example, if the prefix html: is bound to http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml as in , the expression \<html:mark>[class]{highlight}{some highlighted text} will emit the following XML:

In reality, the appropriate @xmlns:html attribute will be attached only where it is needed in keeping with the hierarchical structure of the XML tree. Note that when the same textual prefix is bound to two different namespaces, one of them will be automatically \alpha-renamed so as to result in correct XML with the intended semantics.
The following code causes MathML to be emitted (in the correct namespace), combining and :

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester 4.0 › enhanced XML support › emitting XML and resolving namespaces › XML literals

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The old \xml syntax has been replaced with a new convenient notation for XML literals that supports declared XML namespace prefixes. The grammar of XML literals is summarised below:
For example, if the prefix html: is bound to http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml as in , the expression \<html:mark>[class]{highlight}{some highlighted text} will emit the following XML:

In reality, the appropriate @xmlns:html attribute will be attached only where it is needed in keeping with the hierarchical structure of the XML tree. Note that when the same textual prefix is bound to two different namespaces, one of them will be automatically \alpha-renamed so as to result in correct XML with the intended semantics.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester 4.0 › enhanced XML support › emitting XML and resolving namespaces › XML namespace declaration

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Forester treats XML namespace prefixes in a way that respects \alpha-equivalence: in other words, any prefix mentioned must be bound, and it is possible for two different textually identical namespace prefixes to denote different namespaces depending on their scope. This makes it possible to compose code that emits XML without coordinating namespace prefixes, which I have found to be a major impediment to the correct use of XML for non-trivial work.
An XML namespace prefix is bound by a declaration like the following:
This declaration has the same scoping rules as \def.
